data.title: Oakmark International Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
First-quarter highlights

Top contributors

  • BNP Paribas (OTCQX:BNPQF)
  • Alibaba Group (BABA)
  • Thyssenkrupp (OTCPK:TYEKF)

Top detractors

  • WPP Group (WPP)
  • Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF)
  • Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF)

New purchases

  • Asahi Group Holdings (OTCPK:ASBRF)
  • Flutter Entertainment (FLUT)
  • Louis Vuitton Moët Hennessy (OTCPK:LVMHF)
  • Mitsubishi Estate (OTCPK:MITEY)
  • Rentokil Initial PLC (RTO)

Final sales

  • Bunzl (OTCPK:BZLFF)
  • Liberty Global Cl A (LBTYA)
  • Smurfit WestRock (SW)
  • Sunrise Cl A ADR

FIRST QUARTER, 2025

  • The Fund’s Investor Share Class returned 7.88% versus 6.20% for the MSCI World ex USA Index for the quarter, and 8.37% since inception versus 6.07% for the index over the same period.
  • At the sector level, financials and health care were the largest contributors to performance, while communication services and information technology were the largest detractors.
  • Geographically, the top three country allocations are 24.2% in Germany, 20.2% in France and 14.8% in the U.K. For the quarter, Japan, Germany and China were contributors to relative performance. France, Switzerland and Spain were detractors from relative performance. Emerging markets accounted for 8.1% of the portfolio.

TOP CONTRIBUTOR | DETRACTOR

Top contributor

BNP Paribas was the top contributor during the quarter. The France-headquartered bank’s stock price rose throughout the period as it posted strong fiscal-year 2024 results. Performance was driven by strength in the Corporate and Institutional Banking (CIB) and Personal Finance segments. The company also demonstrated improved efficiency, with revenue growth outpacing cost growth. Given BNP’s strong underlying profitability and highly diversified business, we believe the bank is wellpositioned for sustained capital generation and long-term growth.

Top detractor

WPP Group was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.K.-headquartered advertising company’s stock price declined after it delivered weaker than expected fourth-quarter 2024 results and 2025 guidance. The results were impacted by client losses, disruptions from restructuring activity, and pullback in more discretionary client spending. Despite the headwinds, margins and free cash flow (FCF) remained resilient. While the economic environment remains uncertain, management believes WPP’s growth should start to recover in the back half of 2025, and their FCF generation should rebound as restructuring activities fade. While the past year has been a tough one for WPP, we continue to believe the business can deliver low single-digit growth and much improved FCF. WPP can also monetize equity assets like Kantar for material amounts. We continue to think the discounted valuation overly discounts the company’s fundamental value.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

Given attractive valuation levels across most international markets it was an active quarter for portfolio additions. We established five new positions including two Japan-based businesses.

New purchases

Asahi Group Holdings is a leading global beverage company and Japan's largest beer company. In our view, the Japanese beer market will see tailwinds over the coming years as a result of liquor tax reform and price increases following years of decline. With a strong CEO in Atsushi Katsuki and the country’s best-selling beer brand, we believe the company is uniquely positioned to capitalize on these tailwinds and expand domestic profits over the medium term. In addition, we like that Asahi commands attractive market positions in Australia and Central Europe, which support high margins and revenue growth. Lastly, after years of deleveraging following large acquisitions, we expect management to increase shareholder returns while continuing to realize the benefits of the company’s global procurement initiatives. Despite this compelling setup, we were able to purchase shares in this above-average business at a below-average valuation, in our view.

Flutter Entertainment is the world's largest online gambling company, boasting a portfolio of premier online sports betting and casino brands including FanDuel, Sportsbet, Pokerstars and Paddy Power. The company commands the number one market position across the vast majority of its revenue base driven by its best-in-class product. We appreciate that online gambling favors scaled players and believe that management has Flutter well-positioned to capitalize on strong secular growth tailwinds as the penetration of online gambling continues to increase. A recent sell-off in the share price provided the opportunity to buy shares in a company led by a management team that is renowned for top-notch execution and capital allocation.

Louis Vuitton Moët Hennessy (LVMH) is the world's largest luxury goods company with a portfolio of 75 brands, including prestigious houses Louis Vuitton and Christian Dior. Importantly, the company is focused on fashion and leather goods. This is one of the more attractive subcategories within the sector and where Vuitton has high brand desirability and scale, which drive industry leading profitability. The business is run by Bernard Arnault, who we consider to be one of the best CEOs in Europe, and who is fully aligned with shareholders given his family’s 41% ownership. The opportunity exists because of the recent fears around tariffs and the macro environment (initially in China and increasingly in the U.S.). We view this as a rare opportunity to purchase shares in LVMH at a discounted valuation to its peers and the company's own trading history. We expect that the industry will recover over the medium term, that recent innovations will help sustain attractive revenue growth and that self-help will serve to reaccelerate earnings growth.

Mitsubishi Estate is the largest owner of real estate in Tokyo's Marunouchi neighborhood. Marunouchi is the prime business district in Tokyo where office rental space has historically been characterized by low vacancy rates and rising rents, even in deflationary environments. In our view, Japan's shift to an inflationary environment will provide additional tailwinds to Mitsubishi's growing rental income in Marunouchi. We also like that the company has been strategically monetizing assets in other regions and returning funds to shareholders through buybacks and dividends, helping to boost the company's return on equity. We were happy to recently purchase shares at a discount to our estimate of net asset value and private market comparable transactions. We believe the gap between share price and intrinsic value should narrow as capital is returned to shareholders.

Rentokil Initial PLC is the largest pest control company globally. We think pest control is a good business that grows GDP plus with low volatility, earns attractive margins, and has low capital intensity with strong cash flow generation. As a market leader, Rentokil additionally benefits from scale advantages that drive lower procurement and customer acquisition costs, and greater customer monetization, thanks to the breadth of products and services the company can offer at a premium price. Due to industry fragmentation, Rentokil also has a strong pipeline of acquisitions with attractive economics. While most of Rentokil’s mergers and acquisitions are bolt-ons, they have occasionally done large deals. Their most recent large deal, the acquisition of Terminix, has gone poorly to-date and resulted in a share price decline in excess of the deal value. While Terminix’s integration will take several quarters to complete, we believe they will ultimately succeed and that the resulting business will be a stronger and more profitable one that can deliver growth in line with the industry's average. This should drive a re-rating in the shares towards peer and private market multiples. We believe the market has overly discounted the business’s future prospects.

PORTFOLIO MANAGERS

(Year joined Harris | Oakmark)

David G. Herro, CFA (1992)

Michael L. Manelli, CFA (2005)*

Eric Liu, CFA (2009)

*Effective July 1, 2025 Tony Coniaris will replace Michael Manelli as a portfolio manager of the Fund.

Expense ratios are as of the Fund’s most recent prospectus dated 1/28/2025, as amended and restated 1/31/2025 and 3/14/2025; actual expenses may vary. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Since inception returns for the indexes are calculated based on the Investor Class inception date.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The performance data quoted represents past performance. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Total return includes change in share prices and, in each case, includes reinvestment of dividends and capital gain distributions. The investment return and principal value vary so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost.

The securities mentioned above comprise the following percentages of the Oakmark International Fund’s total net assets as of 03/31/2025: Alibaba Group 1.5%, Asahi Group Holdings 0.3%, BNP Paribas 3.7%, Bunzl 0%, Flutter Entertainment 0.7%, Glencore 2.4%, Kering 3.1%, Liberty Global Cl A 0%, LVMH 0.2%, Mitsubishi Estate 0.1%, Rentokil Initial 0.1%, Smurfit WestRock 0%, Sunrise Cl A ADR 0%, thyssenkrupp 0.8% and WPP 1.7%. Portfolio holdings are subject to change without notice and are not intended as recommendations of individual stocks.

Access the full list of holdings for the Oakmark International Fund as of the most recent quarter-end.

The information, data, analyses, and opinions presented herein (including current investment themes, the portfolio managers’ research and investment process, and portfolio characteristics) are for informational purposes only and represent the investments and views of the portfolio managers and Harris Associates L.P. as of the date written and are subject to change and may change based on market and other conditions without notice.

This content is not a recommendation of or an offer to buy or sell a security and is not warranted to be correct, complete or accurate.

Certain comments herein are based on current expectations and are considered “forward-looking statements.” These forward looking statements reflect assumptions and analyses made by the portfolio managers and Harris Associates L.P. based on their experience and perception of historical trends, current conditions, expected future developments, and other factors they believe are relevant. Actual future results are subject to a number of investment and other risks and may prove to be different from expectations. Readers are cautioned not to place undue reliance on the forwardlooking statements.

The MSCI World ex USA Index ('net') is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure international developed market equity performance, excluding the U.S. The index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each country. This benchmark calculates reinvested dividends net of withholding taxes. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

The MSCI EAFE Index ('net') is designed to represent the performance of large and mid-cap securities across 21 Developed Markets countries in Europe, Australasia and the Far East, excluding the U.S. and Canada. The Index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each of the 21 countries. This benchmark calculates reinvested dividends net of withholding taxes. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

On occasion, Harris may determine, based on its analysis of a particular multi-national issuer, that a country classification different from MSCI best reflects the issuer’s country of investment risk. In these instances, reports with country weights and performance attribution will differ from reports using MSCI classifications. Harris uses its own country classifications in its reporting processes, and these classifications are reflected in the included materials.

The Fund’s portfolio tends to be invested in a relatively small number of stocks. As a result, the appreciation or depreciation of any one security held by the Fund will have a greater impact on the Fund’s net asset value than it would if the Fund invested in a larger number of securities. Although that strategy has the potential to generate attractive returns over time, it also increases the Fund’s volatility.

Investing in foreign securities presents risks that in some ways may be greater than U.S. investments. Those risks include: currency fluctuation; different regulation, accounting standards, trading practices and levels of available information; generally higher transaction costs; and political risks.

All information provided is as of 03/31/2025 unless otherwise specified.

Before investing in any Oakmark Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, management fees and other expenses. This and other important information is contained in a Fund's prospectus and summary prospectus. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing. For more information, please visit Oakmark.com or call 1-800-OAKMARK (1-800625-6275).

Natixis Distribution, LLC (Member FINRA | SIPC), a limited purpose broker-dealer and the distributor of various registered investment companies for which advisory services are provided by affiliates of Natixis Investment Managers, is a marketing agent for the Oakmark Funds.

Harris Associates Securities L.P., Distributor, Member FINRA.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Launch, Lurch, Lift-Off Cycle
data.description:
Stocks rose today, closing out one of the most head-spinning weeks in recent memory - coming off an equally nutty one last week. By the closing bell, the S&P 500 had booked its biggest weekly gain since October 2023 and the Nasdaq since November 2022.

Level Change 4/11/25 (%)

+1.6 Dow

+2.1 Nasdaq

+1.9 Nasdaq 100

+1.8 S&P 500

+1.4 S&P 400

+1.3 S&P 600

The latest trade war salvo came from China, which threw sharp elbows against America’s 145% tariff on Chinese goods with a 125% broadside of its own. At least it mentioned it will ignore future US increases, which seem to appear more often than bedbugs in a budget motel.

Chinese leader Xi Jinping said his country is “not afraid,” and China’s Commerce Ministry waved off Washington’s ever-escalating tariff theatrics as “nothing more than a numbers game, with no real economic significance.” It ridiculed Team Trump’s weaponization of tariffs “as a tool of bullying and coercion, turning itself into a joke.”

Although there does seem to be some economic significance going around.

US Treasuries are going pale in the face as the world begins to question whether America’s economic steering wheel is attached to anything at all. Investors dumped US government bonds this week, driving prices lower and - by the laws of inverse correlation between price and yield - yields higher. The 30-year yield posted its biggest weekly jump since 1982.

Instead of US government bonds, the new safe haven appears to be German and Japanese bonds. This switcheroo coincides with the dollar’s steady trip fall the stairs (down more than 3% this week), suggesting the era of US exceptionalism may soon get its closing credits.

Things are getting so nervy that the Fed needed to assure everyone it will step in if markets seize or liquidity dries up.

Boston Fed President Susan Collins repeated the do-nothing official stance on rates, but added music to the message. She said the Fed has tools for market dysfunction and low liquidity, and is “absolutely prepared to move.” Investors liked the sound of that, and handed her partial credit for today’s bounce.

New York Fed President John Williams expects US economic growth to slip under 1% this year, as the trade war drives up inflation and unemployment. Still, it’s go-slow for him and all the Fedsters on the tightrope strung over a fog machine.

Meanwhile, economic data keep arriving like bills after a vacation: unwelcome and piling up.

April preliminary Michigan consumer sentiment missed expectations and notched its fourth straight monthly decline. Inflation expectations rose, with the one-year up 1.7 percentage points to 6.7%, a big jump from 5% in March to its highest since 1981.

Longer-term (5 to 10 years) edged up 0.3 percentage points to 4.4%, quietly undermining the Fed’s narrative that long-term inflation expectations remain “well-anchored.” The anchor, it seems, is sliding through sand, and the boat’s drifting steadily from the Fed’s 2% harbor.

Maybe the real hard data will come to the rescue. Q1 earnings season started today, and so far, so good. JPMorgan (JPM +4%) grew profit 9%, while Wells Fargo (WFC -1%) grew it 6%. Morgan Stanley (MS +1.4%) beat earnings expectations. BlackRock (BLK +2.3%) hit record assets of $11.6T. Apparently, someone out there still has money.

JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon noted that clients are becoming “more cautious” as geopolitical and trade tensions rise. They seem to dislike inflation, too. He warned that “the economy is facing considerable turbulence,” and his bank is braced “for a wide range of scenarios.” Some of them, one hopes, don’t involve helmets and barf bags.

Those are the winds of Wall Street tonight, where tariffs bite and bond traders blink harder than someone biting into wasabi they thought was guac.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Undervalued Yen Is Also One Of The Few 'Safe Havens'
data.description:
In today's unsettling macroeconomic environment, where even US Treasury Bonds no longer seem like a safe haven, few assets remain uncorrelated to the broader market. While predicting currency movements can be challenging, the Japanese Yen stands out as a potential beneficiary in this market turmoil and geopolitical context. Additionally, the wide interest rate differential between U.S. and Japanese interest rates, which has been a key factor holding back the Yen, may be nearing its end, potentially paving the way for a reversal in the currency’s trajectory. I recommend taking a long position on the JPY/USD currency pair as part of a long-term or medium-term strategy.

Investors shouldn't expect high double-digit returns, as the objective of this trade is mainly protective. For those without access to foreign exchange or interest rate derivatives, ETFs like NYSE:"FXY" (with a modest expense ratio of 0.4%) provide a practical vehicle to gain exposure.

Background and currency valuation methods

After decades of the Yen strengthening against the USD, which started because of the Japanese economic miracle (1960s), since 2010, the yen has weakened against the USD. Ultra-loose monetary policy by the Bank of Japan (BOJ) to foster its export-driven economy contributed to the Yen weakness. This has been exacerbated by the Fed's tightening stance since 2021, which intensified carry-trade dynamics (investors borrowing in yen and investing in higher-yielding USD bonds). Despite the BOJ's interventions, the yen continued to depreciate, unlike the Swiss franc, which appreciated. As a result, Japanese goods and services have become extremely cheap in USD terms, with Tokyo now more affordable than many major Latin American cities.

USD/JPY (Japanese yens per USD) 1971 – 2025

In this article, I will be using the two main theories used to value currencies: Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) and Interest Rate Parity (IRP).

PPP is an economic theory that suggests that in the long run, exchange rates should adjust so that a basket of goods costs the same in different countries when measured in a common currency. As baskets of goods differ by countries, The Economist uses the McDonald's Big-Mac burger to calculate currency valuations in different parts of the world.

The second most used approach is IRP. This theory states that currency forward (exchange rate in the future) will depend on the spread between interest rates (as per the formula below). If bonds from Country A offer a 2% higher yield than those from Country B, IRP predicts that Country A’s currency should depreciate by approximately 2%. Otherwise, arbitrage opportunities would emerge.

PPP and IRP: reality check

Is the Yen cheap according to PPP? Absolutely! According to the Economist Big-Mc Index, Japan has the most undervalued currency of the G7 and, together with India, the most of the G20. According to this index, adjusted by GDP per capita, “A Big Mac costs 46.3% less in Tokyo ($3.11) than in the United States ($5.79) at market exchange rates (January). Based on differences in GDP per person, a Big Mac should cost 7.4% less. This suggests the Japanese yen is 42% undervalued.”, according to The Economist Metric.

Purchasing Power Parity (USD to Japanese Yen)

Despite this historically significant undervaluation (according to PPP theory), the interest rate differential between the US and Japan has prevented the Yen from strengthening as capital inflows have continued to enter the US. American successful tech giants, AI revolution has also helped this process. And in the last four years, the US restrictive monetary policy with many FED rate hikes, has fostered even further the Yen weakness. The latter has occurred despite the BOJ having loosened the yield curve control. Also, between 2022-2024, the BoJ spent over USD160 billion in FX interventions to slow the yen’s depreciation, with limited success.

What about IRP? Following Fed tightening in 2021, US short-term (3-year bonds) rates increased, generating a spread of 150bps vs. Japanese bonds. As a result, the yen depreciated from around 100 Yen per USD to almost 160 yen. If this spread narrows, as it happened during the 2009 financial crisis, the Yen is likely to strengthen vs. the dollar.

JPY vs. US/Japan 3-Year Yield Spread favors inflows to US markets

Are we entering a new era where the Yen will revalue against the USD?

The American administration has requested that countries with significant trade surpluses reduce it or face “reciprocal tariffs”. If tariffs cause calamity in financial markets as we have witnessed, a rebalancing of trade deficits is likely to come as well through exchange rates, as it happened in the 1980s. Finally, this will be a much healthier and “clean” solution, avoiding free-market distortions and free-trade treaty violations.

Risks

I am aware that forecasting currency is one of the most difficult tasks in economic history. Particularly in the short term, anything can happen! And sometimes, currencies can remain overvalued or undervalued for extremely long periods of time due to interest rate differentials, capital controls, and many other forces. Betting the Yen against the dollar has been incredibly unsuccessful.

If history serves as a lesson, following the Plaza Hotel Accord in 1985, although both Germany and Japan devalued their currencies, only in the case of Germany was it useful as a tool to reduce the American trade deficit. In the case of Japan, cultural and other factors prevented more buying of American products in Japan.

Also, long-term rates in Japan are low as there is a high demand for fixed-income securities: an aging population requires these assets to fund pensions. Also, an aging population tends to save more and consume less. This creates a “saving glut”, which is a structural factor behind low interest rates in Japan.

Conclusions and final thoughts

I believe that the current trade war started by the American administration should end in some kind of political agreement like the “Plaza Hotel Accord”, where other G7 countries let their currency devalue against the USD to correct imbalances in the 1980s. It is already happening now with the Euro. This time, it could happen with many Asian currencies, which are among the most undervalued currencies in the world.

From an IRP perspective, the US self-inflicted potential recession and price drops in commodities (e.g., oil, copper) will likely lead to a bearish Fed, willing to reduce interest rates more quickly, which could result in USD weakness against the Yen. This could bring USD/JPY towards levels near 100, helping to reduce the American trade and current account deficit with Japan.

If you want to play long YEN/USD and don't have access to currency or interest rate derivatives markets, you can do it through FXY. This ETF has call options, though not very liquid, which are an alternative.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Country ETFs Since Liberation Day And Pause Day
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPY) rallied 5.7% this week but remains down 5.4% since the close on "Liberation Day" on 4/2. Below is a look at the performance of 45 country ETFs traded on US exchanges since 4/2 and since the close on 4/8 before President Trump announced the 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for all countries except China.

The average country ETF is down 4% since 4/2, so SPY has underperformed that since Trump's Rose Garden announcement. Since the close on 4/8 before the pause, the average country ETF has bounced back 7.9%, so SPY has underperformed slightly on the bounce-back as well.

The country ETFs that have bounced the most since the pause are ones you might expect. Asian countries like Vietnam (VNAM) and Thailand (THD) had some of the harshest reciprocal tariffs announced on Liberation Day, and since the pause, these two have bounced back 16.7% and 14.1%, respectively. Thailand (THD) is now down just 1.2% since Liberation Day.

The third-best country ETF since 4/8 has been Argentina (ARGT) with a gain of 12.8%. ARGT gained 6.5% on Friday to end this week.

China - the country most punished by Trump's tariffs - has bounced back for US investors that own the MSCI China ETF (MCHI) since the pause, even though Trump hiked China's tariff rate to 125% on Wednesday. MCHI is up 9.9% since the close on 4/8 versus SPY's gain of 7.5%. Since Liberation Day on 4/2, MCHI is down 8.3%, though, compared to SPY's decline of 5.4%.

There are four country ETFs down less than 1% since Liberation Day: South Korea (EWY), Switzerland (EWL), New Zealand (ENZL), and Belgium (EWK). Colombia (GXG) and Hong Kong (EWH) head into the weekend as the only country ETFs still down 10%+ since 4/2.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Falls Further As Inflation Expectations Soar
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer confidence took another hit this month, primarily due to escalating worries about trade wars, inflation, and the labor market. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index plummeted to 50.8 in April, its second-lowest reading on record, surpassed only by June 2022.

This represents a significant 10.9% (6.2 point) decline from March, undershooting the anticipated 54.0. Furthermore, consumer sentiment has decreased by 34.2% (26.4 points) compared to a year ago, marking the largest annual decline since July 2022.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regards to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month; a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment fell for the fourth straight month, plunging 11% from March. This decline was, like the last month’s, pervasive and unanimous across age, income, education, geographic region, and political affiliation. Sentiment has now lost more than 30% since December 2024 amid growing worries about trade war developments that have oscillated over the course of the year. Consumers report multiple warning signs that raise the risk of recession: expectations for business conditions, personal finances, incomes, inflation, and labor markets all continued to deteriorate this month. The share of consumers expecting unemployment to rise in the year ahead increased for the fifth consecutive month and is now more than double the November 2024 reading and the highest since 2009. This lack of labor market confidence lies in sharp contrast to the past several years, when robust spending was supported primarily by strong labor markets and incomes. Note that interviews for this release were conducted between March 25 and April 8, closing prior to the April 9 tariff partial reversal.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 50.8, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 39.9% below its average reading of 84.5 (arithmetic mean) and 39.1% below its geometric mean of 83.4, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 0th percentile of the 568 monthly data points in this series.

This indicator is somewhat volatile, with an average monthly change of 3.1 points. The latest data shows a 6.2-point decrease from the previous month. To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In April, the CECI dropped to 56.5, the lowest reading since June 2022. This represents an 11.4% decline from the previous month and a 28.5% decline from a year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 61.5.

Meanwhile, the CEI fell to 47.2, the lowest reading since 1980. This represents a 10.3% decline from the prior month and a 37.9% drop from one year ago. This reading was worse than the forecast of 50.8.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations surged from 5.0% last month to 6.7% this month, the highest reading since 1981 and marking four consecutive months of unusually large increases of 0.5 percentage points or more. This month’s rise was seen across all three political affiliations. Long-run inflation expectations climbed from 4.1% in March to 4.4% in April, reflecting a particularly large jump among independents.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Mr. Market Swings: Why Now Might Be Your Moment
data.description:
Thesis

Just a few days after Trump's new tariffs were announced, the market dropped 15%-its worst two-day performance since 2020. While that kind of drop can make one feel uneasy, it may actually be a great opportunity for the long-term investor to make a move.

In this article, I'll explain why now might be the right time to slowly shift the weights of your portfolio more towards equities. The road ahead may be rocky, and the full effects of the tariffs might take years to play out, but moments like this are often when the long-term investors get ahead by sticking to their plan.

Stock Market History: Past 5 years

Everything began in 2020, when the Federal Reserve started the quantitative easing (QE) program to support the U.S. economy during the COVID-19 crisis. The Fed injected liquidity into our financial system by purchasing large amounts of government securities, leading to lower long-term interest rates and encouraging borrowing and economic growth. However, it came at a significant cost.

The Fed, doubled its assets on the balance sheet, peaking at almost $9T from $3.7T over a span of 2 years. When money is suddenly added to the economy, the risk of inflation grows (sounds familiar?). Indeed, the Fed succeeded to stabilize the economy and the market recovered. However, inflation started growing shortly thereafter, and the Fed decided to reverse the process through a QT program to increase in interest rates and battle inflation. If you ask me if the Fed did its job, I would say that they did.

Of course, inflation took various forms, including a broad increase in overall market prices. Just as any other asset, inflation increased the cyclically adjusted P/E as we can see below.

To assess whether the current market price is reasonable or not, we need to examine the historical average P/E ratio over an extended period.

10 Yrs 20 Yrs Since 1971
Average Shiller P/E 30.77 26.85 21.64

I'm often asked what P/E ratio makes an asset cheap or expensive. But the right question to ask yourself is-what's your long-term investment horizon? That should give you the answer. We can agree that a P/E ratio near 40 is generally unjustifiable, unless the asset is experiencing rapid earnings growth and given that the U.S. Economy grows at roughly 2% after inflation, I would conclude that a P/E of 40 is expensive. But isn't a P/E of 30 expensive as well? I would say that it is, and the current market price does not provide us with a margin of safety just yet.

International Trade War

VIX recently peaked at 50, to remind you, at the Covid-19 crash VIX reached a high of 65. This is happening because of uncertainties of how tariffs will impact the economy. The trade tariffs imposed by the current administration aim to increase tax revenues, and reduce demand on goods of countries that have a trade surplus with the U.S., meaning countries that export more to the U.S. than they import from the U.S. It is worthwhile to analyze the formula that was used to calculate tariffs to further illustrate my point.

In the graph above, "ε" illustrates the price elasticity of demand and "φ" the percentage price of imports increase when a tariff is imposed. These constants are set to "ε" = 4 and "φ" = 0.25, effectively cancelling each other out. In other words, the reciprocal tariff equals Trade Deficit / Imports. The bottom line is, the higher the trade deficit in relation to imports that the U.S. has with a country, the higher the tariff will be.

The table above shows the countries that the U.S. has significant trade deficits. These will be the countries that will experience the highest reciprocal tariff according to the equation, eliminating the trade deficit. Of course, the tariff will not impact all goods equally. Below, I have attached another graph that shows what goods the U.S. imports from these countries.

You should expect that all goods in red will be substantially more expensive for the American consumer if the tariffs are imposed for the long term. However, one needs to understand that the current trade balances are as such for a reason. International relationships are far more complex and have been developed over hundreds of years. Surely, the tariffs intent to support U.S. manufacturing, shrink or eliminate the trade deficit, and generate revenues, but actually, it's not that simple. How other nations will react is unpredictable. Eventually, tariffs will lead to rising inflation, supply shortages (as I will explain shortly), and a possible worldwide recession, not to mention the high unemployment.

The increase in prices will be inevitable, the additional cost needs to be paid by someone. The importer is the one who usually pays the tariff at the boarder, and that cost is eventually passed down to the consumer. On the other hand, the importer can always choose to pay the tariff without passing the cost to the consumer, that however, implies that the company in question will have to absorb the costs, which consequently leads to lower margins, lower cash-flows and a lower valuation.

Additional Risks of Imposed Tariffs

If we assume for a moment that the imposed tariffs "succeed", the production won't begin overnight in the U.S. Let's take OLED screens as an example. There is substantial demand in the U.S. for that good. It is being used in smartphones, smart wearables, TVs, tablets and more. How fast can a factory be built to produce OLED screens at scale? Are there enough skilled workers? We all know the answer to these questions. This shortage of supply will increase prices and to solve this issue, companies will be forced to buy the imported good. So the inflated price will have to be paid no matter what.

We should be reminded that the reason U.S. companies choose imported goods is simply because they are cheaper. The U.S. consistently offers the highest wages for comparable occupations across Europe and Asia. It is evident that prices in the U.S. will be higher compared to the rest of the world. Apple, for example, won't sell their iPhones in Europe for a lower price. My point is that even if a Samsung smartphone would now cost $2,500 for the U.S. consumer, that won't make iPhones any cheaper. Now, we also have to factor in the cost of producing an iPhone in the U.S., which would also lead to a higher price that the consumer would have to pay. No matter what we choose- if tariffs are imposed for a long-period of time, inflation is inevitable.

The Bottom Line

Tariffs are self-inflicted, and I see them as a negotiation tool that is being used between nations to cut a deal that benefits both sides. The negative effects will be immediate, and any potential gains will take years to materialize as the U.S. consumers gradually and slowly adjust to the new normal. One thing is for sure that no one knows at present how this will play out and how long the tariffs will be in effect. Given the current conditions, I believe it's a good moment for the patient long-term investor to slightly tilt their portfolio toward equities, a 60% allocation would be reasonable. The forward P/E ratio is around 20x, implying an earnings yield of about 5%.

Even though the market isn't cheap, it has not yet reached a level that provides us with a true margin of safety. As such, I don't view current market value as compelling enough for aggressive buying (leaving only 10%-20% in cash). Personally, I would consider the market attractive once long-term yields reaches 10%. In the meantime, I plan to gradually increase my exposure to cheap equities that I already own, taking advantage of this price decline.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Inflation Fell In March
data.description:
The good news is that consumer price inflation fell in March to a year-over-year rate of 2.4 percent.

This is down from a 2.8 percent, year-over-year, rate in February.

The drop and the size of the drop surprised almost everyone. Concerns were that inflation would rise in March.

Here is the recent history.

Yes, inflation seems to be coming down, but the inflation that has filled the 2020 calendar has been very excessive from the viewpoint of the Federal Reserve, which would like to see the overall inflation rate at something considerably less than current levels... something closer to 2.0 percent.

And the future seems to include a picture of even higher rates of inflation, given the movement in the world to a situation of much higher tariff rates.

An environment with higher rates of inflation is something that the Federal Reserve might have to deal with in the coming months.

Slowing economic growth may also be a component of the picture the Fed faces.

More and more analysts are painting a world of “stagflation” as what to expect in the near future... rising levels of inflation and slower economic growth.

How the Federal Reserve will react to this new picture is unknown at the present time.

For now, the Federal Reserve has just entered its third round of quantitative tightening, a regime that began with the “first round” getting started around the middle of March in 2022.

This third round of quantitative tightening began two weeks ago, and the Federal Reserve removed $18.4 billion from its portfolio in the banking week ending April 2, 2025, and actually allowed the securities portfolio to rise by $0.4 billion in the latest banking week, the banking week that ended on April 9, 2025.

In the second round of quantitative tightening, the Fed's securities portfolio declined by $257.0 billion. Also, in this second round, the Fed saw about $94.0 billion run off from its loan portfolio.

The Federal Reserve still would like to reduce its securities portfolio by about $2.0 trillion more, but it plans to do this further reduction at a slower pace than during the first and second rounds of the quantitative tightening.

But these plans may never see the "light of day" as the changing economy and the changing picture of government budget maneuvers may result in a future that no one can really see right now.

The quantitative tightening has been going on since the middle of March 2022.

Here is the picture of the reductions that have occurred in the Fed's securities portfolio during the past three years!

Notice that during this time, the securities portfolio has fallen from slightly more than $8.4 trillion to just about $6.4 trillion in the past banking week.

At the end of December 2019, the number of securities held outright by the Federal Reserve totalled just over $3.7 trillion.

So, one can make a strong argument, I believe that the Federal Reserve would still like to remove a substantial number of securities from its securities portfolio.

The amount the Fed would like to reduce the securities is unknown, but I feel that the number Fed officials would be shooting for is still relatively substantial.

Thus, quite a bit of work still remains to get the Fed's balance sheet back to where officials would like it to be.

In terms of the Fed's policy rate of interest, the Federal Funds rate, I believe that Chairman Jay Powell and the Federal Open Market Committee still believe that the time is not right for the Fed to reduce its policy rate of interest anymore in the very near future.

President Trump may not be very happy with this.

And as rising inflationary expectations work to push up interest rates, the Federal Reserve may be done...for the short run...with any near-term future moves in the policy rate.

Remember that the number one policy instrument in how the Fed conducts monetary policy these days is the expansion or reduction in the Fed's portfolio of securities held outright.

The Federal Reserve is on a “quantitative variable” approach to monetary policy.

Movements in the Fed's policy rate of interest follow.

The reluctance to move on adjusting the Fed's policy rate of interest is consistent with the current stance of making Federal Reserve policy.

Thus, I would be surprised if there is any move in the Federal Funds rate in the near future.

But...as mentioned in other parts of my writing...there is so much uncertainty floating around in the financial markets these days that investors must remain "loose and flexible" and keep their eyes open for what seems to be happening.

I believe that the Federal Reserve has done a very good job getting us to where the economy is these days.

My hope is that the Fed will have more time to continue its current policy of quantitative tightening.

I would like to see the Fed continue to reduce the size of its securities portfolio.

Personally, I would like to see the Fed reduce the size of the securities portfolio by another $1.5 to $2.0 trillion. I would also be very happy if the securities portfolio could be reduced by another $1.0 trillion.

I am not sure the Federal Reserve will have the chance to make these numbers. But they are my numbers and I will stand by them.

I believe that Chairman Powell is very intent on moving in this direction.

I believe that Chairman Powell is going to be pushed very hard away from this in the near future.

The history of this period will probably be a very, very interesting piece of reading.

Let's just hope that the outcome of the actions over the next several years does not end up with another major financial disturbance to explain.

data.title: Northland Power: Undervalued By The Market, Resilient To Tariffs And Positioned For Growth
data.description:
Northland power (TSX:NPI:CA) is a Canadian IPP focused on the development, construction, and operation of offshore wind farms. I believe that the recent downturn in the financial markets, triggered by the tariffs imposed by the Trump administration, may represent an excellent entry point for Northland. Indeed, the company has low exposure to the U.S. market, where it only owns a 220 MW onshore wind farm in New York State and has no projects under construction in the short to medium term. Moreover, the company is characterized by strong revenue stability, generating more than 90% of its revenue from contracted sources, a portion of which is indexed to inflation, making it resilient even in potentially adverse economic scenarios.

On the operational side, two huge offshore projects, Hai Long (1 GW) and Baltic Power (1.1 GW), will be completed between 2026 and 2027, which, with Oneida storage battery system, will boost revenue growth and operating income, adding, according to management estimates, about CAD 600 million in adjusted EBITDA and CAD 200 million in adjusted free cash flow. These developments would imply an FY 2027(e) adjusted EBITDA of CAD 1.8 billion, or about 6.5x in comparison to the company's EV.

Although Northland operates in a business characterized by high operational risks, I believe these are already priced in by the market and that the company has a potential upside of 50%, as evidenced by the DCF analysis. For these reasons, I assign Northland Power a "buy" rating.

Business Overview

Northland Power is an IPP with a net installed capacity of 2.84 GW as of December 2024, consisting of offshore and onshore wind farms, photovoltaic plants and gas production facilities. It generated 11 GWh of electricity in FY24, up 6.4% YoY (4% CAGR between FY19 and FY24). The company generates 60% of its revenue in Europe, 23% in Canada, 2% in the US and 15% in Colombia, where it operates through the regulated utility Empresa de Energía de Boyacá.

The company's operations are divided into 4 segments:

Offshore Segment

The Offshore segment had an installed capacity of 902 MW as of December 2024 and accounted for 50.2% of FY 2024 revenue, up 3.7% YoY due to increased electricity generation compared to FY 2023. Despite this, the business unit posted an EBIT margin of 46.6%, lower than the 47.43% recorded in FY 2023, as a result of higher maintenance costs attributable to the outage of an export cable at Gemini in June 2024, which led to a 16% increase in operating costs. The segment represents the company's main investment area and is currently engaged in the construction of two huge wind farms:

Hai Long, located offshore in Taiwan, will have an installed capacity of 1 GW. Completion is expected between FY 2026 and FY 2027, although it should start generating revenue as early as FY 2025. Northland holds a 30.6% ownership interest in the project but continues to take the lead role in the project's construction and operation. Hai Long 2A benefits from a 20-year PPA with Taipower, while Hai Long 2B and 3 have secured a 30-year PPA with an investment grade counterparty (S&P: AA-). The total cost of the project is around CAD 9 billion, of which CAD 5 billion is financed by non-recourse debt, CAD 1 billion from pre-completion revenue, and the remainder through equity investments by the project partners (Northland, Gentari, and Mitsui & Co). Northland's share in the project is expected to generate average adjusted EBITDA between CAD 230 and 250 million.

Baltic Power, located offshore in Poland, has an installed capacity of 1.1 GW and is expected to be completed in FY26. Northland holds a 49% ownership in the project, through a JV with Orlen (51%). The cost of the project is around CAD 6.5 billion and will be remunerated through a 25-year euro-pegged and inflation-indexed CfD with the Government of Poland. Northland's share in the project is forecast to generate an average adjusted EBITDA of between CAD 300 and CAD 320 million.

Onshore Segment

The Onshore business had installed capacity of 968 MW of onshore wind and 247 MW of solar farms as at December 2024. The business unit accounted for 20.3% of Northland's FY 2024 revenue, up 9.9% YoY as a result of the full-year contribution from New York wind projects and higher wind resources, partially offset by lower solar resources in Spain. The segment recorded an EBIT margin of 38.78% in FY 2024, compared to 39.38% in FY23.

In March 2024, the company completed the sale of the 130 MW La Lucha solar facility, for consideration of CAD 205 million. The sale is in line with the strategy of developing onshore assets with the aim of selling them, raising capital, or use the cash generated to finance the development of offshore assets, which require a significant amount of capital but are characterized by higher expected returns.

Northland is completing the construction of the 250 MW Oneida storage system in Ontario, with the facility due for completion in FY25. The project, roughly 70% controlled by the company, generates 60% of its revenue from a 20-year contract with Ontario's Independent Electricity System Operator; the remaining part of the revenue will come from electricity selling at market price. The estimated cost is CAD 800 million and is expected to generate around CAD 40 million of average adjusted EBITDA.

Efficient Natural Gas and Utility Segments

The Efficient Natural Gas segment has an installed capacity of 723 MW and generated 13.5% of the company's revenue in FY24, down 6.4% YoY due to lower natural gas prices and reduced operating availability as a result of plant outages. This segment is the only one with a steady decrease in installed capacity and, hence, in revenue. Despite this, the growing demand for electricity has prompted Northland's management to change its strategy, expecting new strategic investments in the segment. As early as November 2024, it completed a 23 MW capacity expansion at the Thorold gas plant in Ontario, with a CAD 40 million investment.

The decision to pursue new investments in gas facilities will enable the company to leverage its experienced team of gas professionals, using short-term capital to generate cash flows that will fund the development of long-term offshore renewable projects (e.g. ScotWind). The recent appointment of Christine Healy as CEO has given new impetus to this approach. Indeed, in the Q4 conference call, the new CEO highlighted the relevance of gas to meeting the rising demand for base load capacity caused by drivers including data centers, reshoring, and electrification.

Northland acquired Colombian regulated utility EBSA in 2020 with approximately 480,000 customers and supplying 1.8 TWh of electricity annually. The segment accounted for 15.1% of Northland revenue in FY 2024, up 18% YoY. The business unit is the fastest-growing segment in which the company operates, with enhanced performance being primarily driven by growing regulated asset base and rate escalations. These factors led to a steady improvement in operating margins, with the EBIT margin at 32.19% in FY 2024, from 29.12% in FY 2023 and significantly higher than the 24% in FY 2020, the year of acquisition.

What Makes Northland a Compelling Bet Amid Current Economic Conditions

As of April 2, 2025, Northland's market cap declined by approximately 10%. In spite of that, the company's business model is resistant to the impact of tariffs. That is because Northland generates only 2% of its revenue in the US, through an onshore wind farm that is already operating in New York state and owns no projects under construction in the short to medium term, thus requiring no imports into the US. Hai Long and Baltic Power will not require US components as most wind blades, towers, and turbines needed to come from Asia and Denmark, respectively. Even for Alberta's Jurassic solar project, which is in advanced stages of development, components will be imported from Asia.

In the event tariffs lead to a worldwide economic slowdown or recession, Northland would experience little impact on revenue, margins, and cash flows, as over 90% of its revenue is contracted. Additionally, any decline in interest rates due to bad economic conditions could even turn out to be an opportunity as the company would be able to refinance loans with higher interest rates.

Furthermore, the completion of Hai Long, Baltic Power, and Oneida will drive new momentum in revenue and margin growth. Management estimates a cumulative adjusted EBITDA of CAD 600 million from the three projects, forecasting adjusted EBITDA between CAD 1.6 and CAD 1.8 billion in FY27, about 30% higher than in FY24. The two offshore projects will also positively impact margins and profitability, as they feature higher, expected, returns than the onshore ones. This is reflected in the offshore segment’s EBIT margin of 46.6% in FY24, significantly above the 38.78% reported by the onshore segment.

Northland, contrary to some IPPs, is already generating positive free cash flow despite significant investment activity. In FY24, it generated FCF of CAD 651 million (including the sale of La Lucha). The completion of the new projects will contribute an additional CAD 200 million in adjusted FCF, allowing the company to keep debt under control (already stable over the FY20–FY24 period) and to maintain a high level of shareholder remuneration, with a dividend yield of 6.56%. Management also announced that starting with the April 2025 dividend, the dividend reinvestment plan will be executed through open market purchases, minimizing shareholder dilution.

In my opinion, a final factor that makes Northland an attractive investment is its low valuation, in fundamental terms, compared to some of the major peers. The comparison provided by Seeking Alpha with Ormat Technologies (ORA), ReNew Energy Global (RNW), Enlight Renewable Energy (ENLT), Clearway Energy (CWEN) and NextEra Energy (NEE) highlights Northland's relative undervaluation compared to its competitors. Indeed, the Canadian company shows a lower P/E, EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA than all the other companies under review.

Main Risks

Northland operates in a highly regulated and subsidized industry. Regulatory changes in its main countries of operation (Germany, Netherlands, Canada and Colombia) may negatively impact its bottom line.

Northland generated 15.1% of its revenue and 11.9% of its adjusted EBITDA in Colombia in FY24, a country characterized by high political risk. In addition, operations in the country also entail a risk of exposure to currency volatility relative to the Canadian dollar.

Dealing with the construction of large projects, the biggest operational risk for the company is poor planning, which can lead to delays in project completion, cost overruns, and unexpected technical challenges.

The war in Ukraine has simultaneously highlighted the centrality and fragility of energy infrastructure. Though the risk is low for the moment, an eventual war between China and Taiwan could pose a very high risk to the Hai Long offshore wind farm, with a significant impact on Northland's future economic performance.

Northland, which deals with large offshore wind farms, requires substantial amounts of capital. An inability to raise it or high interest rates could negatively impact its business model.

Discounted Cash Flow

To attribute a valuation to Northland Power, I performed a three-stage DCF. I made an estimate of the expected values of revenue, EBIT margin, CAPEX and depreciation for FY 2025, FY 2026, FY 2027. I made a projection of these values for the period between FY 2028 and FY 2034, finally calculating the terminal value. This analysis returned a target price of CAD 26.66 per share, which is about 50% higher than the current market price. To determine this value, I made the following assumptions:

Beta was obtained using the unlevered beta of "Green & Renewable Energy" industry reported by Professor Aswath Damodaran, subsequently calculating levered beta based on NPI's D/E ratio. A value of 0.96 was derived.

Cost of debt of 3.5%.

Risk-free rate of 4.92%, based on the average of the 10-year government bonds of the countries where NPI operates.

Equity risk premium of 4.29% was obtained from the weighted average of the ERPs of the countries where NPI operates. Countries' ERPs are provided by Aswath Damodaran. Cost of equity of 9.02% derived from CAPM model.

WACC of 6.3%.

The growth rate (g) of 2%.

Takeaway

I believe that Northland Power's stock price has been excessively penalized by investors, as the company has minimal exposure to tariffs and is covered by more than 90% contracted revenue in the event of an economic slowdown or recession. The company continues to invest significant amounts of capital in offshore projects, diversifying its renewable assets and reducing their concentration. The completion of Hai Long and Baltic Power will lead to a considerable increase in operating margins and cash flows, enabling the company to keep debt under control, gradually increase its dividend, and begin the development and construction of new offshore projects, a sector in which it is among the top players worldwide. Considering the results of the DCF analysis, which indicate a potential upside of 50%, I assign a "buy" rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UTG: Big Steady Income, Healthy AI Price Pullback
data.description:
The Reaves Utility Income Trust (NYSE:UTG) is a special utility sector-focused closed-end fund offering big, tax-advantaged, monthly income (7.6% yield) and unique exposure to the AI datacenter megatrend, which has just pulled back and thereby offers some compelling price appreciation potential (not to mention it also trades at a small but attractive discount to net asset value (NAV). This report reviews the fund’s strategy, holdings, distribution safety, valuation (and megatrend exposure) and risks, and then concludes with our strong opinion on investing.

Overview

With an inception date in 2004, and according to the fund’s website, the objective of UTG is (emphasis ours):

“To provide a high level of after-tax income and total return, consisting primarily of tax-advantaged dividend income and capital appreciation. The Fund pursues this objective by investing at least 80% of its total assets in dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and debt instruments of companies within the utility industry. Up to 20% of the Fund may be invested in the securities of companies in other industries.”

And as you can see in the chart above, UTG has accomplished this objective by delivering steady growing monthly income distributions (dividends) to investors through a combination of long-term capital gains (taxed at the lower long-term capital gains tax rate, depending on your personal tax situation) and qualified dividends from its recently 59 underlying holdings (qualified dividends are also eligible for the lower qualified dividend tax rate, unlike some other CEF distributions, and depending on your personal tax situation—such as what is your income tax rate, and if you hold the shares in a taxable account or in a tax-advantaged individual retirement account).

As mentioned above, at least 80% of UTG’s total assets must be invested in dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and debt instruments of companies within the utility industry. Here's a look at the current holdings breakdown (as per UTG’s most recent quarterly fact sheet).

Specifically, the fund had 60.6% of its holdings in the Utilities “industry.” This may seem to violate the 80% utility industry rule mentioned above, but per a previous voice message we received from the company:

"Hey,... calling from Reaves Asset Management, we're the advisor of the Reaves Utility Income Fund. Appreciated your question on utility allocations... We use a utility definition that is a little looser for the bulk of the assets as described in the prospectus."

For more color, you can see the fund’s recent top 10 holdings below (many of which have attractive exposure to the AI/datacenter megatrend—more on this later).

(TLN) (VST) (CEG) (NI) (EPD) (PPL) (ETR) (PNW) (TXNM)

Also, for more perspective, here's a look at the fund’s recent holdings breakdown across stocks, bonds and cash.

You’ll note in the graphic above, cash is negative, indicating leverage (or borrowed money), a topic we will cover more later in this report.

7 Big-Yield CEF Questions to Always Ask

Before investing in any big-yield CEF, here's a list of seven specific questions you may always want to ask yourself.

Does UTG Match Your Investment Goals:

Every investor is unique with their own individual goals and situation. UTG can help a lot of investors achieve their goals if the fund’s objectives (i.e., to provide a high level of after-tax income and total return consisting primarily of tax-advantaged dividend income and capital appreciation) match their personal situation.

For example, if you like big, steady monthly income payments, you might consider owning some UTG.

However, it’s important to consider the fund’s specific holdings and megatrend exposure. For instance, in UTG’s case:

Utility Sector: Traditionally, the utilities sector is known for relatively low-price volatility and relatively high dividend income. This is because utility companies (such as the electric company that allows you to keep the lights on) are a steady business (even when the economy gets bad—people still need electricity to keep the lights on, charge their devices, etc.). So, in this sense, UTG is a steady business, and that's further supported by the fund’s relatively low three-year beta of 0.59 (beta is a measure of market risk exposure, whereby the S&P 500, for example, is the baseline with a beta of 1.0, and anything below 1.0 is considered less volatile as compared to the rest of the market, per se).

AI/Datacenter Megatrend: Investors should also be aware of the fund’s exposure to the AI megatrend through datacenter electricity demand. Specifically, the utilities sector is expected to grow significantly over the next decade (a good thing) because of the increasing demand for electricity needed to power AI, which is run through offsite cloud-based data centers. To give you an idea of just how large this growing demand is, the following chart suggests datacenter electricity demand (as a percent of total US electricity demand) will grow from around 5% to around 12% over the next decade. This is huge and good for UTG considering it owns names like Talen, Vistra and NiSource (see our earlier top 10 holdings chart).

Distribution Safety:

Investors can get a feel for the safety of UTG’s distribution first by recognizing its consistency (and growth) since the fund’s 2004 inception (see our earlier distribution history chart).

Second, the table below shows the majority of the distribution is from income (received from the funds underlying holdings) and capital gains (again on the fund’s underlying holdings) and includes only a small amount of ROC (return of capital) recently, which is basically when the fund returns some of an investor’s investment dollar just to maintain the distribution (which is totally fine, from time to time, as long as it is not excessive—which it has not been).

Further still, you can see the ongoing dividend growth of the fund’s underlying holdings, using this link, which shows, on average, the underlying holdings increased their dividend payouts by over 10% last year—a good thing!).

Price Discount to NAV

Unlike traditional mutual funds and exchange trade funds, CEFs can trade at large premiums and/or discounts to the aggregate value of their underlying holdings (because there is no immediate mechanism in place to prevent this), and as such, this can create significant risks and opportunities.

All else equal, we greatly prefer to purchase attractive CEFs when they are trading at a discount to NAV, which UTG currently is (a small one of ~1.1%).

And for a little more perspective, here's a look at UTG’s share price and dividend paid over time.

As you can see above, the dividend has grown steadily over time (it now sits at $0.19 per share each month) and the price has also grown (although recent price volatility has created a more attractive buying opportunity, especially considering the ongoing utility sector megatrend related to datacenter/AI and considering the underlying holdings have continued to increase their dividends paid over time, especially over the last year, as mentioned earlier).

Risks:

Before investing in UTG, investors should also consider the risks.

Leverage: Closed-end funds frequently use a little leverage (or borrowed money) to increase the distributions and total returns over time. This can be attractive in the good times, painful in the bad times, but potentially very good over the long term.

UTG recently had around 19.6% leverage, which is very reasonable considering the low volatility and high dividend payout of the fund (remember, utility sector stocks tend to be less volatile, and UTG’s beta is still well below 1.0, even including the leverage).

Further still, the shares have sold off recently (perhaps a bit more than they would have if there was no leverage). And while this creates some pain for existing investors, it also creates a more attractive entry point for new investors, in our opinion.

Expenses: CEF expenses are a common complaint of CEF investors. For example, some investors are turned off by UTG’s recent expense ratio of ~2.43%.

Important to note, this expense ratio includes the cost of leverage and other operating expenses, plus the investment advisor’s fee. In our view, the total expense ratio of this fund is meaningful but reasonable and acceptable considering the strategy.

The management team is another factor (risk) for investors to consider. However, in the case of UTG, we view the management team as reasonably good stewards of capital. For example, they have a well-defined investment philosophy and disciplined investment process. And the management team has an average of 20 years of experience.

The Bottom Line

We consider UTG attractive enough to rank it in our new report: Top 7 Big Yields (CEF and BDC Edition). In particular, the current market selloff has pulled down the price, yet its distribution and holdings remain steady and healthy.

Additionally, UTG trades at a small discount to NAV (attractive), it has exposure to the AI/datacenter megatrend (electricity demand is growing) and it's significantly less exposed to market volatility (it has a low 0.59 beta) as compared to other opportunities (potentially a very good thing in the current market environment, and if you're simply at a point in life where you prefer to focus on steady income above long-term volatile price appreciation).

UTG is currently a significant holding in our 23-position High Income Now Portfolio at our Investing Group service, Big Dividends PLUS.

At the end of the day, you need to do what is right for you. And if you're a disciplined, long-term, income-focused investor, UTG is absolutely worth considering.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Costco Amidst Tariff Risks: To Have Or To Hold?
data.description:
Thesis

For the membership-based warehouse supermarket leader Costco Wholesale Corporation (NASDAQ:COST), the potential impacts of President Trump's tariffs leave more questions than answers, and investors should carefully navigate the period ahead as recession risks remain, and retaliatory tariffs threaten to impact supply chains, especially from China, and product inflation. Costco is considered a defensive stock, as most of its revenue is derived from less discretionary purchases, but it will not be immune from tariffs entirely.

With the company priced highly – in relation to its peers and its historical averages – and with growth in revenue expected to stagnate somewhat, I view COST shares as presently overvalued. Costco is a hugely successful business with a loyal customer base, and investors with a long-term horizon will continue to enjoy decent returns, but I expect to see a more favorable entry point emerge soon. I rate shares a hold.

Company Background and Performance

Costco has been a bellwether for American economic growth for decades. It has a long track record of cash generation as a business and enjoys many competitive advantages: a profitable business model based on both product sales and membership fees; massive economies of scale; and a strong brand and loyal customer base. While it does not enjoy a huge amount of pricing power in relation to its consumer goods – given that big box retail stores and supermarkets operate in a very competitive marketplace where customers look for value and closely track prices – it does enjoy enormous pricing power for its membership fees, from where it derives much of its profit. This positions it in a different space than regular retail stores and supermarket chains, where margins are thin. It has dominant power in this specific sub-section of consumer spending.

Costco's investors with a long-term focus have been richly rewarded, and I expect they will continue to be over coming years and decades. Investors have demonstrated trust in the management and shown they are willing to pay a premium for a company that takes the stress away from investing, and a look at its stock chart over an extended period shows this clearly.

The stability of their business and reassurances of consistent profitability – even if a low to mid-teens gross margin – comes at a cost, however. Costco is richly valued, with a P/E ratio of 53, a forward PE of 47.9 and EPS growth rates in the very low double digits, it has a current PEG ratio of 5.4. This positions it above its peers – Walmart (WMT), Kroger (KR) and Target (TGT) – but each of them has higher gross profit margins; Costco's is at 12.7% compared to the mid/high-20s for the others. Moreover, both Kroger and Walmart have superior EPS growth prospects according to analysts.

The impact of Trump's sweeping tariffs has added further factors for investors to consider here. With so many "moving parts," investors are wise to assess possible outcomes before jumping head-first into owning equity in any business right now, even a consistently profitable defensive stock like Costco.

Potential Tariff Impacts

I see two potential outcomes from Trump's tariffs. Firstly, Costco will be forced to raise its prices but, theoretically, not across the board and instead focused only on certain items. In fact, Reader's Digest recently reported several consumables subject to higher prices.

According to CNN, imported food items represent approximately 17% of American consumption, but in certain areas, significantly more: 80% of seafood; 80% of coffee; 59% of fresh fruit; and 35% of vegetables. The U.S., due to crop and climate issues, cannot hope to produce every food item that American consumers want to eat, so to think that American farmers can produce mangoes or catch shrimp at the high volumes necessary to meet American consumption needs is simply unreasonable. If Americans want to continue having their rich variety of food options, international trade is necessary. Tariffs will hurt in this regard, and force Costco and its peers to either raise prices, leading to customer churn or spending reductions, or accept lower profit margins.

Impacts would be spread across both its (non-discretionary) grocery and (discretionary) houseware businesses. Regarding the former, Costco is in a very favorable position compared to smaller, less agile peers. It can potentially absorb wholesale price increases with its strong product mix. Costco could theoretically take a loss on certain items, knowing that it can make this up somewhere else. The CEO Ron Vachris stated in the latest earnings call:

...our people are very well equipped to lower prices and defer any cost increase that come our way.

He also reiterated their strong relations with suppliers, which they can leverage better than their smaller competitors. This means that they could continue to attract customers and steal market share from local grocers, who have less flexibility in their pricing models. Over the long term, it is possible that tariffs improve their market share by crippling their smaller competitors.

Costco's exposure to Chinese imports, especially in the household items, means they are not entirely protected, however. Recently, Costco asked its suppliers in China to cut prices, which represents another lever they can pull. According to Vachris, less than half of the 1/3rd of company sales that come from imported products – so, less than 1/6th of product revenue – derives from China, Mexico and Canada.

While products from China are currently subject to 145% import tariffs (as of April 11), many products from Mexico and Canada are exempt from the 25% tariffs on imports from these two countries. This includes food and agricultural products, as they are covered by the existing USMCA free-trade agreement. So, when you look closely, the proportion of products likely impacted by tariffs from China is probably less than 10%, though the proportion from other countries – and subject to the sweeping 10% tariffs that exist right now – is likely higher.

In the latest earnings call, Vachris stated in his prepared remarks:

It is difficult to predict the impact of tariffs, but our team remains agile and our goal will be to minimize the impact of related cost increases to our members.

They have a number of options at their disposal to do so, but some degree of price inflation – which may hurt consumer spending – and falling margins is to be expected. While Costco is positioned strongly in this challenging macroeconomic environment, they are certainly not immune to tariffs.

A second possible outcome is that the tariffs lead to a recession, which, when mixed with inflation – and, therefore, an inability for the Fed to lower interest rates – could also lead to rising unemployment and an economy characterized by stagflation. This would be a terrible outcome, and the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act of 1930 – signed by President Hoover, amidst bipartisan opposition, to protect American jobs in manufacturing and farming – serves as a cautionary tale for what could happen. Reciprocal tariffs led to a 70% global slowdown of trade; businesses were crushed, families were cast into poverty, markets tumbled. Such a result in 2025/26 is not beyond the realms of possibility, though the President does have the option of reversing course, albeit with some sustained erosion of general "trust" in the U.S. as a global trading partner.

Speaking of reciprocal tariffs, the Chinese recently responded with 125% tariffs on U.S. goods imported to China. Aside from the potential global slow-down of trade that this tit-for-tat competition between the U.S. and China may accelerate, there is also the question of how these reciprocal tariffs will impact American businesses that sell to the Chinese. Costco has seven stores in China – representing less than 1% of its 903 in total – so any material reduction in revenue would be minimal. Without knowing exactly what proportion of goods sold in these stores is based in the U.S. – though I suspect somewhere in the region of 20-40% – this will almost certainly hurt the company's same-store sales.

Also, investors should anticipate growing anti-U.S. sentiment among consumers in China and elsewhere. Costco has 280 stores outside of the U.S. (incl. 109 in Canada and 41 in Mexico), which constitute 31% of revenues. They will not be immune to the shifting winds of socio-political impacts and consumer spending if a revolt/boycott of sorts materializes.

Conclusion

Costco stock has traded better than the S&P 500 (SP500) of late, driven by the "flight to safety" that often precedes a likely recession or follows a growing collective fear of one. This may provide some stability to the share price going forward, but I would expect it to remain under pressure given its historically high valuations, seen in its five-year P/E-ratio chart below.

Any sign that Costco is facing pressure on its profit margins would likely lead to another drawdown. While its solid balance sheet and wide moat will protect the company over the long term, I do not view shares as good value right now. I rate Costco a hold.
